title: AI Governance Awareness Training
description: Mandatory governance training covering AI system awareness, limitations, human oversight, risk management, incident reporting, and governance responsibilities. Aligned with the AI Training and Competence Policy and ISO/IEC 42001 requirements.

sections.title: Introduction to AI Governance
sections.content:
## Why AI Governance Exists

Artificial intelligence systems are increasingly used across professional services to support analysis, decision-making, and operational efficiency. While these systems offer significant benefits, they also introduce risks that must be managed responsibly.

AI governance exists to ensure that the organisation uses AI systems in a way that is safe, ethical, transparent, and aligned with professional and regulatory expectations.

### Risks of Automated Reasoning Systems

Automated reasoning systems can produce outputs that appear authoritative but may be incorrect, incomplete, or biased. Without appropriate governance:

- Decisions may be made based on unreliable information
- Regulatory obligations may be breached
- Clients and stakeholders may be exposed to harm
- The organisation's professional reputation may be damaged

### Professional Accountability

AI governance reinforces a fundamental principle: **AI systems support professional work but do not replace professional judgement.**

Every person in the organisation has a responsibility to engage critically with AI outputs and to escalate concerns when they arise. Governance is not a bureaucratic exercise — it is a professional obligation.

### Our Governance Framework

Gibbs Consulting maintains an AI Management System (AIMS) aligned with ISO/IEC 42001. This framework includes:

- An AI Governance Policy setting out our commitments
- A risk management framework for identifying and mitigating AI risks
- Defined roles and responsibilities for AI governance
- Procedures for incident reporting, monitoring, and human oversight
- This training programme to ensure awareness and competence

sections.title: AI Systems Used by the Organisation
sections.content:
## Understanding Our AI Landscape

Gibbs Consulting uses AI systems in various capacities across the business. Understanding where AI appears in your working environment is an important part of governance awareness.

### Types of AI Systems

The organisation may use the following categories of AI system:

- **Large Language Model (LLM) Applications** — AI tools that generate, summarise, or analyse text. These may be used for drafting documents, answering questions, or supporting research.
- **Analytical Tools** — Systems that process data to identify patterns, trends, or anomalies. These support operational and strategic analysis.
- **Decision Support Systems** — AI tools that provide recommendations or assessments to support human decision-making.
- **Machine Learning Models** — Systems trained on historical data to make predictions or classifications.

### Where AI Appears in Operations

AI systems may be present in:

- Internal analysis and reporting workflows
- Client-facing advisory and consulting engagements
- Research and development activities
- Operational efficiency tools and automation

### The AI System Inventory

All AI systems used by the organisation are recorded in the **AI System Inventory**, which is maintained within the AIMS application. Each system is classified by:

- Risk level (Low, Medium, or High)
- Deployment status (Development, Testing, Active, Suspended, or Retired)
- Environment (Research, Development, Internal Analysis, Production, or Consulting)
- Human oversight requirements

If you become aware of an AI system being used within the organisation that is not recorded in the inventory, you should report this to the AI Governance Lead.

sections.title: Limitations of AI Systems
sections.content:
## Understanding What AI Cannot Do

AI systems, including the most advanced language models, have significant practical limitations that all staff must understand.

### Plausible but Inaccurate Outputs

AI systems can generate outputs that appear confident, well-structured, and authoritative — but are factually incorrect. This is sometimes referred to as 'hallucination'. An AI system may:

- Cite sources that do not exist
- Present fabricated statistics as fact
- Provide logical-sounding explanations that are fundamentally wrong

**You cannot assume an AI output is correct simply because it looks professional.**

### Misinterpretation of Incomplete Data

AI systems may draw incorrect conclusions when provided with incomplete, ambiguous, or poorly structured input data. They do not understand context in the way a human professional does.

### Inconsistent Responses

The same question posed to an AI system at different times may produce different answers. AI outputs are not deterministic in many implementations, meaning they can vary between runs.

### Behaviour Outside Intended Context

AI systems are designed and tested for specific use cases. When used outside their intended context, they may produce unexpected, unreliable, or harmful outputs.

### The Professional Scrutiny Requirement

All AI outputs used in professional work must be subject to appropriate scrutiny. This means:

- Verifying factual claims against authoritative sources
- Checking calculations and data analysis independently
- Assessing whether the output is reasonable given the context
- Not presenting AI-generated content as your own work without review

**AI outputs require the same professional scrutiny you would apply to any other source of information.**

sections.title: Human Oversight Expectations
sections.content:
## The Qualified Human in the Loop

Gibbs Consulting operates a **Qualified Human in the Loop** principle for all AI systems. This means that a suitably qualified person must review and take responsibility for decisions that rely on AI outputs.

### Core Principles

**Humans remain responsible for decisions.** AI systems provide inputs, suggestions, or analysis — but the decision and accountability rests with the human professional. You cannot delegate professional responsibility to an AI system.

**AI outputs must be reviewed where appropriate.** The level of review should be proportionate to the risk and impact of the decision. Higher-risk decisions require more rigorous review.

**Reviewers must challenge unreliable outputs.** If an AI output seems unusual, inconsistent, or potentially incorrect, it is your responsibility to investigate further rather than accept it at face value.

**Automated systems must not override professional judgement.** Where an AI system makes a recommendation that conflicts with your professional assessment, your judgement takes precedence. Document the rationale for your decision.

### Levels of Oversight

The organisation applies different levels of human oversight depending on the risk classification of the AI system:

- **Standard Review** — Routine checking of AI outputs before use (Low risk systems)
- **Enhanced Review** — Detailed scrutiny with documented rationale (Medium risk systems)
- **Specialist Review** — Expert assessment with formal sign-off (High risk systems)

### Your Responsibilities

Regardless of your role, if you use AI system outputs in your work you are expected to:

- Review outputs before relying on them
- Apply professional judgement to assess their quality
- Document your review where the context requires it
- Escalate concerns about AI system reliability

sections.title: AI Risk Awareness
sections.content:
## Understanding AI Risk

The organisation maintains a structured approach to identifying, assessing, and managing risks associated with AI systems. All staff should understand the types of risk that AI systems may introduce.

### Categories of AI Risk

**Incorrect Outputs** — AI systems may produce wrong answers, flawed analysis, or misleading recommendations. If these are relied upon without review, they can lead to poor decisions and professional liability.

**Biased or Misleading Results** — AI systems may reflect biases present in their training data or design. This can lead to outputs that are unfair, discriminatory, or systematically skewed in ways that are not immediately obvious.

**Operational Disruption** — Dependence on AI systems creates operational risk. If an AI system becomes unavailable, produces degraded outputs, or behaves unexpectedly, business operations may be affected.

**Regulatory Exposure** — The use of AI in professional services is subject to evolving regulatory expectations. Non-compliance with emerging standards can expose the organisation to legal and regulatory risk.

**Data Risks** — AI systems may process sensitive or confidential data. Inappropriate data handling can lead to privacy breaches, confidentiality violations, or data protection non-compliance.

**Misuse** — AI systems may be used for purposes beyond their intended scope, or in ways that do not align with the organisation's values and policies.

### How Risks Are Managed

AI risks are managed through the **AI Risk Management Framework**, which includes:

- Risk identification and assessment for each AI system
- Risk ratings based on likelihood and impact
- Mitigation measures documented in the AI Risk Register
- Regular review cycles to reassess risks
- Escalation procedures for critical or emerging risks

If you identify a new risk or believe an existing risk assessment is inadequate, raise this with the AI Governance Lead or your AI System Owner.

sections.title: Incident Reporting
sections.content:
## Reporting AI Concerns and Incidents

The organisation maintains an AI incident reporting process to ensure that concerns about AI system behaviour are captured, investigated, and resolved.

### What Constitutes an AI Incident

An AI incident is any event where an AI system produces an outcome that causes or could cause harm, disruption, or concern. This includes:

- **Misleading outputs** — The AI system produced information that was materially incorrect and could have led to a wrong decision
- **Unexpected behaviour** — The AI system behaved in a way that was not anticipated or was outside its normal operating parameters
- **Unusual results** — Outputs that seem inconsistent with the input data or the expected use case
- **Data concerns** — Situations where the AI system may have processed data inappropriately, exposed confidential information, or used incorrect data sources
- **Bias indicators** — Outputs that appear to reflect systematic bias or unfairness

### How to Report

AI incidents should be reported through the **AIMS application** using the Incidents module. When reporting an incident, include:

- Which AI system was involved
- What happened (description of the incident)
- When it occurred
- What the potential impact was or could have been
- Any immediate actions you took

### Severity Levels

Incidents are classified by severity:

- **Low** — Minor issue with limited impact, easily corrected
- **Medium** — Noticeable issue that affected work quality or required rework
- **High** — Significant issue that affected a client deliverable or operational decision
- **Critical** — Serious issue with potential for significant harm, regulatory breach, or reputational damage

### The Reporting Culture

The organisation encourages open and blame-free reporting of AI concerns. Reporting an incident is a professional responsibility, not an admission of fault. Early reporting enables the organisation to learn, adapt, and improve its governance.

**If in doubt, report it.** It is better to raise a concern that turns out to be minor than to overlook an issue that escalates.

sections.title: Governance Responsibilities
sections.content:
## Shared Governance Responsibility

AI governance is not the sole responsibility of a single team or individual. It is a shared responsibility that applies to everyone who interacts with AI systems within the organisation.

### Key Governance Roles

**AI Governance Lead** — Has overall responsibility for the AI Management System, including policy development, risk oversight, and ensuring the organisation meets its governance obligations.

**Technical Architecture Lead** — Responsible for the technical assessment and inventory of AI systems, including system design, deployment, and lifecycle management.

**Data Governance Lead** — Oversees data quality, data protection, and the appropriate use of data within AI systems.

**AI System Owner** — Each AI system has a designated owner who is accountable for the system's governance, risk management, and operational performance.

**Operational Reviewer** — Conducts regular reviews of AI system outputs and performance, documenting findings and escalating concerns.

### What This Means for You

Regardless of your specific role, you are expected to:

- **Be aware** of the AI systems relevant to your work
- **Apply oversight** by reviewing AI outputs before relying on them
- **Report concerns** through the incident reporting process
- **Maintain competence** by completing governance training and staying informed
- **Follow policies** as set out in the AI Governance Policy and related documents
- **Support governance activities** such as audits, reviews, and monitoring when requested

### Governance as Professional Practice

AI governance is an extension of the professional standards that the organisation already upholds. It is about applying the same rigour, accountability, and ethical standards to AI-assisted work as we apply to all professional services.

By completing this training, you are contributing to the organisation's governance culture and demonstrating your commitment to responsible AI use.

### Further Information

All governance documents, including policies, procedures, and frameworks, are available in the Policy Documents section of the AIMS application. If you have questions about your governance responsibilities, contact the AI Governance Lead.

title: AI Ethics & Responsible Use
description: Training on the ethical principles underpinning responsible AI use, covering fairness, transparency, accountability, non-discrimination, and human dignity. Aligned with ISO/IEC 42001 Annex A controls on AI policies and governance.

sections.title: Ethical Principles for AI
sections.content:
## Why Ethics Matter in AI

AI systems can have significant impacts on individuals, communities, and society. Ethical principles provide a framework for ensuring that AI is developed and used in ways that respect human rights, promote fairness, and avoid harm.

Gibbs Consulting is committed to the responsible use of AI in all its operations. This commitment goes beyond legal compliance — it reflects our professional values and our duty of care to clients, employees, and the wider community.

### Core Ethical Principles

The organisation's approach to AI ethics is built on the following principles:

- **Fairness** — AI systems should not create or reinforce unfair bias or discrimination against individuals or groups
- **Transparency** — The use of AI should be open and explainable, with stakeholders informed about how and why AI is used
- **Accountability** — There must always be a human who is responsible for decisions made with the assistance of AI
- **Beneficence** — AI should be used to benefit people and the organisation, not to cause harm
- **Human Dignity** — AI systems must respect the rights, autonomy, and dignity of all individuals

### Ethical Decision-Making

When working with AI systems, consider the following questions:

- Could the output of this AI system negatively affect any individual or group?
- Am I comfortable explaining how this AI was used to a client or regulator?
- Would a reasonable person consider this use of AI to be fair and appropriate?
- Have I applied my own professional judgement rather than deferring entirely to the AI?

If the answer to any of these questions raises concern, pause and seek guidance from the AI Governance Lead.

sections.title: Fairness and Non-Discrimination
sections.content:
## Preventing Bias in AI-Assisted Work

AI systems can reflect and amplify biases present in their training data, design, or the way they are used. Understanding bias is essential for all staff who interact with AI outputs.

### Types of AI Bias

**Historical Bias** — AI systems trained on historical data may perpetuate past inequalities. For example, if historical hiring data reflects gender imbalance, an AI trained on that data may favour one gender over another.

**Representation Bias** — If the data used to train an AI system does not adequately represent all groups, the system may perform poorly or unfairly for underrepresented groups.

**Measurement Bias** — The way data is collected or measured can introduce systematic errors that affect AI outputs.

**Confirmation Bias in Use** — Users may selectively accept AI outputs that confirm their existing beliefs while ignoring outputs that challenge them.

### Your Role in Preventing Bias

As a user of AI systems, you can help prevent biased outcomes by:

- Being aware that AI outputs may contain hidden biases
- Questioning outputs that seem to favour or disadvantage particular groups
- Reporting any patterns of potentially biased output through the incident reporting process
- Not using AI systems to make decisions about individuals without appropriate human review
- Ensuring that AI-assisted analysis considers diverse perspectives and data sources

### Protected Characteristics

Particular vigilance is required when AI outputs relate to or could affect individuals based on protected characteristics, including age, disability, gender, race, religion, sexual orientation, or any other characteristic protected by law.

**If you suspect an AI system is producing biased outputs, report it immediately. Do not use the output.**

sections.title: Transparency and Explainability
sections.content:
## Being Open About AI Use

Transparency is a cornerstone of responsible AI governance. Stakeholders have a right to know when AI is being used and to understand how it influences decisions that affect them.

### Transparency Obligations

The organisation is committed to transparency in its use of AI. This means:

- **Disclosure** — Clients and stakeholders should be informed when AI systems have been used in work that affects them, where appropriate
- **Explainability** — Where possible, the reasoning behind AI-assisted decisions should be explainable in terms that non-technical stakeholders can understand
- **Documentation** — The use of AI in professional work should be documented so that decisions can be reviewed and audited

### Explainability in Practice

Not all AI systems are equally explainable. Some systems, such as simple rule-based models, are highly transparent. Others, such as deep learning models, may be more opaque. The organisation manages this by:

- Classifying AI systems by their level of explainability
- Applying higher levels of human oversight to less explainable systems
- Requiring documentation of how AI outputs were used in decision-making
- Maintaining the ability to provide a human explanation for any AI-assisted decision

### What This Means for You

- Do not present AI-generated work as entirely your own without appropriate disclosure
- If a client asks whether AI was used, answer honestly and in accordance with the organisation's disclosure policy
- Document your use of AI systems in your work, particularly for significant decisions or deliverables
- If you cannot explain why an AI system produced a particular output, seek additional review before relying on it

sections.title: Accountability and Professional Standards
sections.content:
## Maintaining Accountability in an AI-Enabled Workplace

The use of AI does not diminish professional accountability. Every individual remains responsible for the quality, accuracy, and appropriateness of their work, regardless of whether AI tools were used in its production.

### The Accountability Principle

AI systems are tools. They do not have professional qualifications, ethical obligations, or legal responsibilities. **You do.** When you use an AI system to support your work:

- You are responsible for verifying the output
- You are accountable for decisions made based on that output
- You cannot transfer blame to an AI system for errors in your work
- Your professional standards apply to AI-assisted work just as they do to any other work

### Professional Standards and AI

The use of AI must be consistent with the professional standards and codes of conduct that apply to your role. This includes:

- **Competence** — You should understand the capabilities and limitations of the AI tools you use
- **Due diligence** — AI outputs should be checked with the same rigour as any other source
- **Confidentiality** — AI tools must not be used in ways that compromise client or organisational confidentiality
- **Integrity** — AI should not be used to mislead clients, stakeholders, or colleagues

### Ethical Escalation

If you encounter a situation where the use of AI raises ethical concerns that you are unsure how to resolve, escalate the matter. The organisation provides clear escalation routes:

- Speak to your line manager
- Contact the AI Governance Lead
- Use the incident reporting process in the AIMS application
- Raise concerns through the organisation's whistleblowing procedure if necessary

**Ethical concerns about AI use will always be taken seriously and investigated appropriately.**

title: Data Handling & Privacy for AI Systems
description: Training on the responsible handling of data when working with AI systems, covering data protection, confidentiality, GDPR considerations, and safe data practices. Aligned with ISO/IEC 42001 Annex A data management controls.

sections.title: Data Protection Principles
sections.content:
## Protecting Data in an AI Context

AI systems rely on data to function. The data you provide to an AI system, and the outputs it generates, must be handled with the same care and professionalism as any other data within the organisation.

### Why Data Protection Matters for AI

AI systems introduce specific data risks that all staff must understand:

- **Data ingestion** — Information entered into an AI system may be stored, processed, or used in ways that are not immediately apparent
- **Data leakage** — Sensitive information provided to an AI system could potentially be exposed to unauthorised parties
- **Data persistence** — Some AI systems may retain input data for model training or improvement purposes
- **Cross-contamination** — Data from one context may inadvertently influence AI outputs in another context

### Core Data Principles for AI Use

When working with AI systems, apply the following principles:

- **Minimisation** — Only provide the minimum data necessary for the task. Do not input more information than the AI system needs
- **Purpose limitation** — Use data only for the purpose for which it was collected and for which you have authorisation
- **Confidentiality** — Do not input confidential client data, personal data, or commercially sensitive information into AI systems unless explicitly authorised
- **Awareness** — Understand where data goes when you input it into an AI system and what happens to it afterwards

### Approved and Unapproved Systems

The organisation maintains a list of approved AI systems in the AI System Inventory. Only approved systems should be used for work purposes. Using unapproved AI tools with organisational or client data is a policy violation.

sections.title: Personal Data and GDPR
sections.content:
## AI and Data Protection Regulation

The UK General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR) and the Data Protection Act 2018 impose strict requirements on the processing of personal data. These requirements apply fully to the use of AI systems.

### What Is Personal Data?

Personal data is any information that relates to an identified or identifiable individual. This includes names, email addresses, identification numbers, location data, and any other information that could be used to identify a person directly or indirectly.

### AI-Specific GDPR Considerations

**Lawful basis** — There must be a lawful basis for processing personal data through an AI system. Processing personal data through an AI tool without a lawful basis is a breach of data protection law.

**Automated decision-making** — Under Article 22 of the UK GDPR, individuals have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing that produce legal or similarly significant effects. If an AI system contributes to such decisions, appropriate safeguards must be in place.

**Data subject rights** — Individuals retain their data protection rights when their data is processed by AI systems. This includes the right to access, rectification, erasure, and to object to processing.

**Data Protection Impact Assessments** — Where AI processing is likely to result in a high risk to individuals, a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) must be conducted.

### Practical Rules

- **Never** input personal data into an AI system unless you have confirmed it is approved for that purpose
- **Never** use AI to make decisions about individuals without appropriate human oversight
- **Always** consider whether personal data can be anonymised or pseudonymised before AI processing
- **Report** any suspected personal data breach involving an AI system immediately

sections.title: Confidential and Client Data
sections.content:
## Safeguarding Confidentiality

Gibbs Consulting handles confidential information on behalf of clients and in the course of its own operations. The use of AI systems must not compromise this confidentiality.

### Categories of Sensitive Data

**Client confidential data** — Any information received from or about clients in the course of professional engagements. This includes financial data, strategic plans, proprietary information, and personal data of client employees or customers.

**Commercially sensitive data** — Information about the organisation's own strategies, finances, intellectual property, or competitive position.

**Privileged information** — Legal advice, audit findings, and other information that may be subject to professional privilege.

**Employee data** — Personal and employment information about the organisation's own staff.

### Rules for AI and Confidential Data

- **Do not input client data into external AI systems** unless the system has been specifically approved for that purpose and the client has been informed where required
- **Do not input confidential business data into AI systems** that may store or transmit data externally
- **Do not copy AI outputs containing sensitive data** into unsecured locations
- **Check engagement terms** — Some client contracts may specifically restrict the use of AI in processing their data
- **Use internal AI tools where available** — Where the organisation provides approved internal AI tools, these should be preferred over external alternatives

### What to Do If Data Is Compromised

If you believe confidential data has been inappropriately entered into an AI system or exposed through AI use:

- Stop using the AI system for that task immediately
- Report the incident through the AIMS application
- Notify your line manager and the Data Governance Lead
- Do not attempt to resolve the issue alone — data breaches require a coordinated response

sections.title: Safe Data Practices
sections.content:
## Practical Data Safety When Using AI

This section provides practical guidance on handling data safely when working with AI systems in your day-to-day work.

### Before Using an AI System

Before inputting any data into an AI system, ask yourself:

- Is this AI system on the approved list in the AI System Inventory?
- What type of data am I about to input? Is it personal, confidential, or commercially sensitive?
- Am I authorised to process this data using this AI system?
- Does the AI system's data handling meet the organisation's requirements?
- Could I achieve the same result using less sensitive data or anonymised data?

### Data Anonymisation and Pseudonymisation

Where possible, data should be anonymised or pseudonymised before being processed by AI systems. This reduces the risk of privacy breaches and data protection violations.

- **Anonymisation** — Remove all identifying information so that individuals cannot be identified from the data
- **Pseudonymisation** — Replace identifying information with artificial identifiers, while retaining the ability to re-identify individuals if necessary

### Prompt Hygiene

When using AI systems that accept text prompts or queries:

- Do not include real names, addresses, or other personal identifiers in prompts unless necessary and authorised
- Do not paste entire documents containing sensitive data into AI prompts
- Use generic examples rather than real client data where possible
- Review prompts before submitting them to ensure they do not contain unintended sensitive information

### Data Retention and Deletion

Be aware of how AI systems handle data retention:

- Some AI systems may retain input data for training or improvement purposes
- Understand the data retention policies of any AI system you use
- Where possible, use AI systems that do not retain input data beyond the immediate session
- If in doubt, consult the AI System Inventory or the Data Governance Lead for guidance

title: AI Impact Assessment Awareness
description: Training on understanding and contributing to AI impact assessments, covering the purpose, process, and staff responsibilities. Aligned with ISO/IEC 42001 Annex A controls on AI system impact assessment.

sections.title: What Is an AI Impact Assessment?
sections.content:
## Understanding AI Impact Assessments

An AI Impact Assessment (AIA) is a structured process for identifying and evaluating the potential effects of an AI system on individuals, groups, communities, and society. It is a key requirement of ISO/IEC 42001 and a central element of the organisation's AI Management System.

### Why Impact Assessments Matter

AI systems can have consequences that extend beyond their immediate technical function. An AI system used in consulting may influence business decisions that affect employees, customers, or communities. An analytical tool may produce results that shape strategic direction. A decision support system may contribute to outcomes that affect people's lives.

Impact assessments help the organisation:

- Identify potential harms before they occur
- Evaluate whether the benefits of an AI system justify its risks
- Design appropriate safeguards and mitigations
- Demonstrate due diligence to clients, regulators, and stakeholders
- Meet the requirements of ISO/IEC 42001

### When Is an Impact Assessment Required?

An AI Impact Assessment should be conducted:

- Before deploying a new AI system
- When making significant changes to an existing AI system
- When an AI system is applied to a new use case or context
- When a material incident occurs that suggests the original assessment may be inadequate
- As part of periodic review cycles for active AI systems

The AI Governance Lead is responsible for ensuring that impact assessments are conducted and maintained.

sections.title: The Assessment Process
sections.content:
## How Impact Assessments Are Conducted

The AI Impact Assessment process follows a structured methodology that examines the AI system from multiple perspectives.

### Assessment Dimensions

Each assessment considers the following dimensions of impact:

**Impact on Individuals** — Could the AI system affect the rights, interests, or wellbeing of individuals? This includes impacts on privacy, autonomy, safety, and access to services.

**Impact on Groups and Communities** — Could the AI system disproportionately affect particular groups? This includes considerations of fairness, equity, and potential for discrimination.

**Impact on the Organisation** — What are the operational, reputational, financial, and legal risks to the organisation?

**Impact on Society** — Could the AI system contribute to broader societal harms, such as erosion of trust, concentration of power, or environmental impact?

### Assessment Steps

The assessment process involves:

- **System Description** — Documenting what the AI system does, how it works, and what data it uses
- **Stakeholder Identification** — Identifying who may be affected by the AI system
- **Risk Identification** — Identifying potential negative impacts across all dimensions
- **Likelihood and Severity Evaluation** — Assessing how likely each impact is and how serious it would be
- **Mitigation Planning** — Designing controls and safeguards to reduce identified risks
- **Residual Risk Assessment** — Evaluating whether remaining risks are acceptable
- **Review and Approval** — Formal sign-off by the AI Governance Lead and relevant stakeholders

### Documentation

All AI Impact Assessments are documented and stored within the AIMS application. They form part of the governance evidence base and may be reviewed during internal or external audits.

sections.title: Categories of Impact
sections.content:
## Understanding Different Types of Impact

AI systems can create a range of impacts that vary in nature, severity, and who they affect. Understanding these categories helps ensure that assessments are thorough and that no significant impact is overlooked.

### Rights and Freedoms

AI systems may affect fundamental rights and freedoms, including:

- **Privacy** — Through the collection, processing, or inference of personal information
- **Non-discrimination** — Through biased outputs that disadvantage particular groups
- **Autonomy** — By influencing or constraining choices without individuals being aware
- **Due process** — By contributing to decisions that affect individuals without adequate transparency or recourse

### Safety and Wellbeing

AI systems may create safety risks, particularly when:

- Outputs are used in safety-critical contexts without adequate verification
- System failures lead to operational disruption with safety implications
- Users develop excessive trust in AI outputs and reduce their own vigilance

### Economic Impact

AI systems may have economic consequences for individuals or groups, such as:

- Affecting employment decisions or opportunities
- Influencing financial assessments or credit decisions
- Shaping business strategies that affect market participants

### Environmental Impact

AI systems, particularly large-scale models, consume significant computational resources. The environmental impact of AI use, including energy consumption and carbon emissions, is a relevant consideration in impact assessments.

### Cumulative and Systemic Impacts

Individual AI systems may have modest impacts in isolation, but their cumulative effect across the organisation or industry may be significant. Impact assessments should consider whether the AI system contributes to broader systemic trends.

sections.title: Your Role in Impact Assessment
sections.content:
## Contributing to Impact Assessments

While formal impact assessments are led by the AI Governance Lead, every member of staff has a role in ensuring that AI impacts are identified and managed.

### How You Can Contribute

**Raise awareness of new AI use.** If you become aware that an AI system is being used in a new way or for a new purpose, report this to the AI Governance Lead. A new or updated impact assessment may be required.

**Provide operational insight.** Impact assessments benefit from the knowledge of people who work with AI systems every day. If you are asked to contribute to an assessment, share your practical experience of how the system works and where its limitations lie.

**Report unexpected impacts.** If you observe that an AI system is having effects that were not anticipated in its original assessment — whether positive or negative — report this through the incident reporting process.

**Think critically about impact.** When using AI systems in your work, consider who might be affected by the outputs and whether those effects are appropriate. This critical thinking is the first line of defence against unintended harm.

### When to Escalate

You should escalate to the AI Governance Lead if:

- You become aware of an AI system being used without an impact assessment
- You believe an existing impact assessment does not adequately reflect the real-world effects of the system
- You identify a potential impact on vulnerable individuals or groups
- You are asked to use an AI system in a way that you believe could cause harm

### Impact Assessment and Continuous Improvement

Impact assessments are not one-off exercises. They are reviewed and updated as part of the organisation's continuous improvement process. Your feedback and observations are an essential input to this process.

title: Third-Party AI Systems
description: Training on the governance requirements for using external and third-party AI systems, covering approval processes, acceptable use, and supply chain considerations. Aligned with ISO/IEC 42001 Annex A controls on third-party relationships and use of AI systems.

sections.title: Understanding Third-Party AI
sections.content:
## What Are Third-Party AI Systems?

Third-party AI systems are AI tools, services, or platforms provided by external organisations that the organisation may use in its operations. These include both commercially licensed products and freely available tools.

### Examples of Third-Party AI

- **General-purpose AI assistants** — Tools such as ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Microsoft Copilot, Claude, and similar platforms that provide conversational AI capabilities
- **Embedded AI features** — AI capabilities built into existing software products, such as AI-powered search, auto-complete, summarisation, or analytics features within business tools
- **Specialist AI services** — AI tools designed for specific professional tasks such as document analysis, translation, code generation, or data processing
- **AI APIs and platforms** — Cloud-based AI services that the organisation integrates into its own systems and workflows

### Why Third-Party AI Requires Special Governance

Third-party AI systems introduce additional governance considerations because:

- The organisation has limited control over how the system processes data
- Data may be transferred to, stored in, or processed in jurisdictions with different legal requirements
- The provider may change the system's behaviour, capabilities, or terms of use without notice
- The system's training data, model architecture, and decision-making processes may not be transparent
- Security vulnerabilities in the provider's systems could affect the organisation's data

sections.title: The Approval Process
sections.content:
## Getting AI Systems Approved for Use

All third-party AI systems must be assessed and approved before they are used for work purposes. Using unapproved AI systems with organisational or client data is a policy violation.

### The Approval Workflow

The process for approving a new third-party AI system involves:

- **Request** — A member of staff identifies a third-party AI system that could benefit their work and submits a request to the AI Governance Lead
- **Assessment** — The AI Governance Lead, working with the Technical Architecture Lead and Data Governance Lead, assesses the system against the organisation's requirements
- **Risk Evaluation** — The system is evaluated for data handling, security, privacy, reliability, and ethical risks
- **Terms Review** — The provider's terms of service, data processing agreements, and privacy policies are reviewed
- **Impact Assessment** — An AI Impact Assessment is conducted to evaluate potential effects
- **Decision** — The AI Governance Lead approves, conditionally approves, or rejects the system
- **Registration** — Approved systems are added to the AI System Inventory with appropriate controls documented

### Conditions of Approval

Approval may come with conditions, such as:

- Restrictions on the types of data that may be processed
- Requirements for human oversight of outputs
- Limitations on use cases or contexts
- Periodic review requirements
- Mandatory training for users

### Checking Approval Status

Before using any third-party AI system, check the AI System Inventory in the AIMS application. If a system is not listed, it has not been approved. If you are unsure, contact the AI Governance Lead.

sections.title: Acceptable Use of External AI
sections.content:
## Rules for Using Third-Party AI Systems

Once a third-party AI system has been approved, it must be used in accordance with the organisation's acceptable use requirements.

### General Rules

- **Use only approved systems** — Do not use AI systems that are not listed in the AI System Inventory
- **Respect data restrictions** — Only input data types that the system has been approved to process
- **Follow use case limitations** — Only use the system for the purposes for which it has been approved
- **Apply human oversight** — Review all outputs before relying on them, as required by the human oversight framework
- **Do not create accounts with organisational email** on unapproved AI platforms

### What You Must Not Do

- **Do not input client confidential data** into any third-party AI system unless it has been specifically approved for that data type
- **Do not use personal AI accounts** for work purposes, even if the underlying tool is approved in its enterprise version
- **Do not share AI system credentials** with colleagues or third parties
- **Do not bypass access controls** or use workarounds to access AI systems that have been restricted
- **Do not rely on AI outputs** from unapproved systems for any professional deliverable

### Free and Open AI Tools

Freely available AI tools, including browser extensions, mobile apps, and open-source models, are subject to the same approval requirements as commercial products. The fact that a tool is free does not exempt it from governance requirements.

### Reporting Unapproved Use

If you become aware that an unapproved AI system is being used within the organisation, report this to the AI Governance Lead. This is not about blame — it is about ensuring appropriate governance is in place.

sections.title: Supply Chain and Provider Management
sections.content:
## Managing AI Providers and Suppliers

ISO/IEC 42001 requires the organisation to manage its relationships with AI providers and suppliers. While this is primarily the responsibility of governance leads, all staff should understand the principles involved.

### Provider Due Diligence

Before an AI provider's system is approved, the organisation evaluates:

- **Data processing practices** — Where is data processed and stored? What are the retention and deletion policies?
- **Security posture** — What security certifications and controls does the provider maintain?
- **Compliance** — Does the provider comply with relevant regulations, including UK GDPR and industry-specific requirements?
- **Transparency** — Does the provider disclose how their AI models work, what data they were trained on, and how they handle user inputs?
- **Contractual protections** — Are there appropriate data processing agreements, service level agreements, and liability provisions in place?

### Ongoing Monitoring

Approved AI providers are subject to ongoing monitoring, which includes:

- Reviewing changes to the provider's terms of service or data handling practices
- Monitoring for security incidents or data breaches at the provider
- Assessing the impact of model updates or changes in system behaviour
- Periodic re-evaluation of the provider against the organisation's requirements

### Your Role

As a user of third-party AI systems, you can support provider management by:

- Reporting any changes in the behaviour or performance of AI systems you use
- Flagging any communications from AI providers about changes to terms or features
- Reporting any concerns about the security or reliability of an AI provider
- Following the approved configuration and settings for each AI system

**Remember: even approved AI systems require ongoing vigilance. Approval is not a permanent guarantee that a system will always meet the organisation's requirements.**

title: AI System Lifecycle Management
description: Training on the stages of the AI system lifecycle, covering design, development, deployment, monitoring, and retirement. Aligned with ISO/IEC 42001 Annex A controls on AI system lifecycle management.

sections.title: The AI System Lifecycle
sections.content:
## Understanding the AI System Lifecycle

Every AI system used by the organisation goes through a defined lifecycle, from initial planning through to eventual retirement. Understanding this lifecycle helps you appreciate the governance controls that apply at each stage and your role within them.

### Lifecycle Stages

The AI system lifecycle consists of the following stages:

- **Planning and Design** — Defining the purpose, requirements, and expected behaviour of the AI system
- **Development and Training** — Building, configuring, or customising the AI system, including training it on appropriate data
- **Testing and Validation** — Verifying that the AI system performs as intended and meets quality and safety requirements
- **Deployment** — Putting the AI system into operational use within the organisation
- **Monitoring and Review** — Ongoing oversight of the AI system's performance, accuracy, and impact
- **Maintenance and Update** — Making changes, improvements, or corrections to the AI system
- **Retirement** — Decommissioning the AI system when it is no longer needed or no longer meets requirements

### Why Lifecycle Management Matters

AI systems are not static. They may change over time due to updates from providers, changes in the data they process, shifts in the operational context, or evolving regulatory requirements. Lifecycle management ensures that governance controls remain appropriate throughout the system's life.

### The AI System Inventory

Each AI system in the organisation's inventory includes a recorded deployment status that reflects its current lifecycle stage: Development, Testing, Active, Suspended, or Retired. This allows the organisation to maintain visibility over all AI systems at all times.

sections.title: Deployment and Change Management
sections.content:
## Deploying AI Systems Safely

The deployment of a new AI system, or a significant change to an existing one, is a controlled process that requires governance oversight.

### Pre-Deployment Requirements

Before an AI system is deployed into operational use, the following must be completed:

- **Impact Assessment** — An AI Impact Assessment must have been conducted and approved
- **Risk Assessment** — All identified risks must have documented mitigations in place
- **Testing** — The system must have been tested in a representative environment and validated against its requirements
- **Data Governance** — Data sources, data quality, and data handling procedures must be confirmed
- **Human Oversight** — The required level of human oversight must be defined and the responsible individuals identified
- **Documentation** — System documentation, user guidance, and operational procedures must be in place
- **Training** — Users must have received appropriate training on the system

### Change Management

When changes are made to an active AI system, the same governance rigour applies. Changes that require reassessment include:

- Updates to the AI model or algorithm
- Changes to the data sources or data processing pipeline
- Expansion of the system's use to new contexts or use cases
- Changes to the provider's terms of service or data handling practices
- Modifications to the system's configuration or parameters

### Your Role in Deployment and Change

If you are involved in deploying or changing an AI system, ensure that the governance process has been followed. If you are a user of an AI system that has been changed, be alert for any differences in behaviour and report anything unexpected.

sections.title: Monitoring and Performance Review
sections.content:
## Ongoing Monitoring of AI Systems

Once deployed, AI systems require continuous monitoring to ensure they continue to perform as expected and that governance controls remain effective.

### What Is Monitored

The organisation monitors AI systems across several dimensions:

**Performance** — Is the AI system producing outputs of acceptable quality and accuracy? Has performance degraded over time?

**Fairness** — Are there any emerging patterns of bias or discriminatory output that were not present when the system was deployed?

**Reliability** — Is the AI system available and functioning consistently? Are there patterns of errors or failures?

**Compliance** — Does the AI system continue to operate within its approved parameters? Have regulatory requirements changed?

**User Feedback** — What are users reporting about the system's behaviour, usefulness, and any concerns?

### Review Cycles

AI systems are subject to periodic formal reviews, the frequency of which depends on the system's risk classification:

- **Low risk systems** — Reviewed annually
- **Medium risk systems** — Reviewed every six months
- **High risk systems** — Reviewed quarterly

Reviews are documented in the AIMS application and may result in changes to the system's risk classification, operating conditions, or continued use.

### Your Role in Monitoring

Every user of an AI system is a monitor. Your day-to-day experience with AI systems provides valuable information about their real-world performance. You contribute to monitoring by:

- Paying attention to the quality and consistency of AI outputs
- Reporting any degradation in performance or unusual behaviour
- Providing feedback on the system's usefulness and limitations
- Participating in formal review activities when requested

sections.title: Retirement and Decommissioning
sections.content:
## When AI Systems Are Retired

AI systems do not last forever. They may be retired because they are no longer needed, because they no longer meet the organisation's requirements, because they have been replaced by a better alternative, or because the risks of continued use outweigh the benefits.

### Reasons for Retirement

An AI system may be retired when:

- It is no longer fit for purpose or has been superseded
- Ongoing risks are assessed as unacceptable and cannot be adequately mitigated
- The provider discontinues the service or changes terms in an unacceptable way
- Regulatory changes make continued use non-compliant
- The cost of maintaining governance controls exceeds the value delivered by the system

### The Retirement Process

Retiring an AI system involves:

- **Decision and Approval** — The decision to retire a system is made by the AI Governance Lead in consultation with the AI System Owner and relevant stakeholders
- **User Communication** — All users are informed of the retirement, including the timeline and any alternative tools or processes
- **Data Handling** — All data associated with the AI system is handled in accordance with data retention and deletion policies. This includes input data, output data, logs, and configuration data
- **Access Removal** — Access to the system is revoked and any integrations are disconnected
- **Documentation** — The retirement is recorded in the AI System Inventory, including the reasons and the date of decommissioning
- **Lessons Learned** — A brief review is conducted to capture lessons learned from the system's lifecycle

### Your Responsibility

If you are informed that an AI system you use is being retired:

- Stop using the system by the communicated deadline
- Ensure you have saved any necessary outputs or data before access is removed
- Transition to any approved alternative systems as directed
- Report any difficulties with the transition to your line manager or the AI Governance Lead

title: AI Compliance & Consequences of Non-Compliance
description: Training on the legal, regulatory, and organisational consequences of failing to comply with AI governance policies and procedures. Aligned with ISO/IEC 42001 Clause 7.3 awareness requirements.

sections.title: The Regulatory Landscape
sections.content:
## AI Regulation and Compliance

The use of AI is subject to a growing body of regulation and professional standards. Understanding the regulatory landscape helps you appreciate why compliance with the organisation's AI governance policies is essential.

### Key Regulatory Frameworks

**UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018** — Governs the processing of personal data, including by AI systems. Applies to any AI processing that involves personal data of UK residents.

**EU AI Act** — The European Union's comprehensive regulation for AI systems, which classifies AI systems by risk level and imposes requirements accordingly. Relevant where the organisation operates within the EU or serves EU-based clients.

**UK AI Regulation** — The UK government's framework for AI governance, which applies sector-specific regulation through existing regulators. This includes the principles of safety, transparency, fairness, accountability, and contestability.

**Professional Standards** — Industry-specific codes of conduct and professional standards that govern how AI may be used in professional services.

**ISO/IEC 42001** — The international standard for AI Management Systems, which provides the framework for the organisation's AIMS. Compliance with this standard demonstrates best practice in AI governance.

### Why This Matters to You

Regulatory requirements are not abstract concerns — they translate directly into your day-to-day responsibilities. Non-compliance can result in enforcement action, financial penalties, and reputational damage that affects everyone in the organisation.

sections.title: Organisational Consequences
sections.content:
## What Happens When AI Governance Fails

Failure to comply with AI governance policies and procedures can have serious consequences for the organisation. Understanding these consequences reinforces the importance of following the governance framework.

### Regulatory and Legal Consequences

**Financial penalties** — Data protection breaches involving AI can attract significant fines. Under UK GDPR, penalties can reach up to 4% of annual global turnover or £17.5 million, whichever is higher.

**Enforcement action** — Regulators may issue enforcement notices, require the organisation to stop using particular AI systems, or mandate specific remedial actions.

**Legal liability** — If an AI system causes harm to a client or third party due to inadequate governance, the organisation may face legal claims for damages.

**Loss of certification** — Non-compliance with ISO/IEC 42001 could result in the loss of the organisation's AIMS certification, affecting its competitive position and client confidence.

### Reputational Consequences

**Client confidence** — Clients entrust the organisation with sensitive data and important decisions. AI governance failures can erode that trust and lead to loss of business.

**Market reputation** — Public incidents involving AI misuse or failure can attract media attention and damage the organisation's reputation in the market.

**Professional standing** — Governance failures can affect the organisation's standing with professional bodies and industry associations.

### Operational Consequences

**Disruption** — AI governance failures may require urgent remedial action, diverting resources from productive work.

**Increased scrutiny** — Failures typically result in increased regulatory and audit scrutiny, creating additional compliance overhead.

**Restricted AI use** — Governance failures may result in the organisation restricting or suspending AI use until issues are resolved, affecting operational efficiency.

sections.title: Individual Consequences
sections.content:
## Personal Responsibilities and Consequences

AI governance is not only an organisational responsibility — individuals also bear responsibility for their actions when using AI systems.

### Your Personal Obligations

As a member of staff, you are personally responsible for:

- Following the organisation's AI governance policies and procedures
- Completing mandatory AI governance training
- Applying human oversight when using AI system outputs
- Reporting concerns and incidents through the proper channels
- Handling data appropriately when using AI systems
- Not using unapproved AI systems for work purposes

### Consequences of Individual Non-Compliance

**Disciplinary action** — Failure to comply with AI governance policies may result in disciplinary proceedings under the organisation's disciplinary policy. This could range from informal guidance through to formal disciplinary action depending on the severity and nature of the breach.

**Professional consequences** — For individuals subject to professional regulation, AI governance breaches could affect their professional standing, registration, or practising rights.

**Legal personal liability** — In serious cases, individuals may face personal legal liability, particularly where non-compliance involves deliberate misconduct, data protection breaches, or negligence that causes harm.

### The Importance of Good Faith

The organisation recognises that mistakes can happen. The governance framework is designed to minimise the likelihood and impact of errors, not to create a culture of blame. Key distinctions:

- **Genuine mistakes** reported promptly and in good faith will be treated as learning opportunities
- **Negligent behaviour** — failing to follow policies or training despite knowing the requirements — may result in disciplinary action
- **Deliberate misconduct** — intentionally circumventing governance controls — will be treated as a serious disciplinary matter

**The best protection against consequences is compliance: follow the policies, complete the training, apply oversight, and report concerns.**

sections.title: Staying Compliant
sections.content:
## Practical Steps for Ongoing Compliance

Compliance with AI governance is not a one-off exercise. It requires ongoing attention and commitment. This section provides practical guidance for staying compliant in your day-to-day work.

### Your Compliance Checklist

- **Complete all mandatory training** — Ensure your training records are up to date, including this module and any role-specific training
- **Know your policies** — Familiarise yourself with the AI Governance Policy and any policies specific to your role or department. These are available in the Policy Documents section of the AIMS application
- **Use only approved AI systems** — Check the AI System Inventory before using any AI tool. If in doubt, ask before you use it
- **Handle data carefully** — Follow the data handling rules covered in the Data Handling & Privacy training module
- **Apply human oversight** — Always review AI outputs before relying on them. Document your review where required
- **Report concerns promptly** — Use the incident reporting process in the AIMS application. Early reporting is always better than delayed reporting
- **Stay informed** — Pay attention to communications from the AI Governance Lead about policy updates, new guidance, or changes to approved systems

### Where to Get Help

If you are unsure about any aspect of AI governance compliance, the following resources are available:

- **AI Governance Lead** — For policy questions, governance concerns, and escalations
- **Data Governance Lead** — For data handling and privacy questions
- **Technical Architecture Lead** — For technical questions about AI systems
- **AIMS Application** — For policies, procedures, system inventory, incident reporting, and training records
- **Your Line Manager** — For role-specific guidance and support

### Continuous Improvement

The AI governance framework is continuously evolving to reflect changes in technology, regulation, and best practice. Your feedback and engagement are essential to this process. If you have suggestions for improving governance practices, share them with the AI Governance Lead.

**Compliance is everyone's responsibility. By following the governance framework, you protect the organisation, our clients, and yourself.**
